askNext: Which external actions are recorded?
Does the trace include approvals, inputs, outputs, and state changes?

action-trace.body: Action traces exist because accountability should not depend on the assistant's memory of its own good intentions.
action-trace.example: "The action trace showed the assistant submitted the form after the approval gate, which made the apology shorter and the fix more obvious."
action-trace.misuse: It gets reduced to a transcript. A useful action trace must capture the action, context, authority, result, and side effect, not merely the sentence the assistant wrote afterwards.
action-trace.practicalMeaning: In practice it means logging enough detail to answer who authorised what, which tool or UI element was touched, what changed, and how to reverse or investigate the action.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A record of what the agent actually did, not what it later said it did.

action-trace.whyExists: Agents increasingly do things outside the chat box. Teams need a way to audit, debug, replay, and explain those actions after the fact.

askNext: Does this refer to African Technopreneurs as a company, a retail channel, a media surface, or a community signal?
What specific adoption problem is being solved?
Is the claim about AI, XR, distribution, education, or ecosystem building?
african-techno.body: The name is useful precisely because it is not pure AI vocabulary. It keeps the dictionary honest about the physical, commercial, and local work that has to happen before any new technology becomes normal.
african-techno.example: "The AI strategy sounded grand until African Techno asked who would explain the hardware, train the users, and handle the customer who just wanted the headset to work."
african-techno.misuse: By treating the name as a generic "African innovation" symbol instead of asking what is actually being provided, sold, taught, rented, or built. A local operator is useful because of concrete activity, not because the label photographs well.
african-techno.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the grounded layer between emerging-tech hype and market adoption: hardware access, XR services, retail channels, founder storytelling, and community work that makes new tools less theoretical.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: African Technopreneurs: the practical operating layer where emerging tech meets South African customers, builders, and communities.

african-techno.warningLabel: A local brand is not a strategy by itself. Ask what it helps people actually do.
african-techno.whyExists: It exists because local technology ecosystems need operators who do the unglamorous middle work: sourcing devices, explaining new interfaces, building distribution, convening people, and turning imported platform language into something a South African buyer or builder can actually use.

askNext: What goal is the agent pursuing, and over how many steps?
What tools can it use, and what guardrails sit between intent and execution?
Would a simpler workflow solve the same problem more predictably?
agent.body: Useful agents exist. They just tend to look less like science fiction and more like tightly scoped systems with clear permissions, strong fallbacks, and very little romance.
agent.example: "The support agent was useful not because it had a heroic inner life, but because it could look up the account, draft the response, and ask for approval before sending it."
agent.misuse: It gets abused whenever a chatbot can do more than one thing. A model with a button labelled "send email" is not automatically an agent. Sometimes the word is doing no more than replacing "workflow" because the latter sounds unromantic.
agent.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means orchestration, tool permissions, planning heuristics, state management, timeouts, approvals, and a carefully chosen boundary around what the system is allowed to decide alone.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A workflow customers will pay more for if it sounds self-directed.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A loop with state, tools, and more ways to fail than a normal request-response app.

agent.warningLabel: Frequently used for systems that are really just orchestrated workflows with a better costume.
agent.whyExists: The term exists because some useful tasks are not one-shot question answering. They require sequences: inspect, decide, act, check, retry, stop. "Agent" is the label for packaging those loops into something more autonomous than a single prompt-response exchange.

askNext: Which credentials exist for each agent, workflow, environment, and connector?
Who owns rotation, expiry, revocation, and scope reduction?
Can the team prove which credentials are still needed, or only that removing them feels risky?
agent-credential-sprawl.body: No diagram is attached because the risk is inventory and lifecycle failure, not a single action loop. A picture would make the sprawl look tidier than it is.
agent-credential-sprawl.example: "The pilot ended in March; the agent's calendar, storage, and CRM tokens remained employed until November."
agent-credential-sprawl.misuse: It is dismissed as ordinary secret management. Agent credentials are often bound to dynamic goals, delegated user authority, tool calls, and automated side effects, so stale or overbroad access can do more than merely sit there looking untidy.
agent-credential-sprawl.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventorying agent tokens, mapping them to owners and workflows, narrowing scopes, rotating secrets, expiring delegated grants, separating environments, and revoking the credentials that survived because nobody wanted to break the demo.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: The credential estate created for agents has grown beyond practical ownership.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A drawer of tokens from pilots, connectors, users, and environments that no one wants to touch.

agent-credential-sprawl.warningLabel: Useful agents accumulate credentials unless something deliberately stops them.
agent-credential-sprawl.whyExists: Agents become useful by connecting to systems that require authority. Each connector, workspace, user, environment, vendor, and workflow can produce another credential. Without lifecycle management, the access estate grows faster than the team's memory of why it exists.

askNext: Does the agent have its own enforceable identity?
Who owns that identity, reviews its permissions, and disables it?

agent-identity.body: No diagram is attached because the core distinction is attribution, not flow. The system either knows which non-human actor did the thing, or it is performing forensics with a blindfold.
agent-identity.example: "The assistant had a friendly name, a cheerful avatar, and exactly the same permissions as the intern, which explained both the speed and the incident."
agent-identity.misuse: It gets confused with branding or personality. Naming an assistant "Milo" is not identity management. The useful question is whether systems can enforce what Milo may touch and prove what Milo actually did.
agent-identity.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means non-human principals, scoped roles, owner metadata, credential lifecycle, run-level attribution, policy checks, and logs that do not collapse the agent into whichever user happened to approve the first prompt.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A manageable principal for the agent rather than a borrowed human shadow.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The paperwork required before autonomous software is allowed near anything consequential.

agent-identity.warningLabel: A friendly assistant name is not an access-control boundary.
agent-identity.whyExists: Agents increasingly act across systems, call tools, request data, and trigger side effects. Giving them a recognisable identity lets teams scope access, attribute actions, rotate credentials, review behaviour, and shut down the right actor when something goes wrong.

askNext: Can we reconstruct the full run from goal to final action?
Which model calls, tool calls, approvals, and state changes are visible after a failure?
Does the telemetry help someone fix the workflow, or only admire the smoke?
agent-observability.body: Agent observability is where autonomy loses its press release and gains a receipt.
agent-observability.example: "The agent observability review found that the model was not confused until the tool returned yesterday's account data with today's schema."
agent-observability.misuse: It gets reduced to pretty traces or token charts. A dashboard is not observability if it cannot show what happened, what mattered, who approved it, which tool changed state, and where recovery was still possible.
agent-observability.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means run traces, tool-call logs, prompt and context snapshots, cost breakdowns, approval records, replayable failures, and enough structure for operations to tell whether the agent completed the work or merely produced a confident artefact.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Enough evidence to explain what the agent did after it stops sounding clever.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Traceable runs, useful logs, and fewer postmortems conducted from vibes.

agent-observability.whyExists: Agent workflows fail across several layers at once. A bad result may come from the model, the prompt, a tool, stale context, permission design, retry logic, or the handoff between them. Observability exists because a final answer is not enough evidence to debug the route that produced it.

askNext: What can we inspect after an agent run fails halfway through a task?
Which actions require approval, rollback, or auditability?
Are we measuring task success, tool misuse, latency, and cost in the same place?
agent-ops.body: Every new software category eventually rediscovers operations. Agent ops is the point at which the romance meets the incident review.
agent-ops.example: "The agent ops review revealed that the system was not autonomous so much as confidently unsupervised."
agent-ops.misuse: It gets abused when a tracing dashboard is mistaken for control. Visibility helps, but it does not solve brittle plans, weak tools, or the small issue of giving software enough access to embarrass the company at scale.
agent-ops.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting run histories, replaying failures, scoring outcomes, tracking tool use, setting approvals, and deciding which errors deserve engineering rather than theology.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: The controls and visibility needed to run agent systems without pure faith.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Making the agent's mistakes legible enough to fix them before they become policy.

agent-ops.warningLabel: Instrumentation is not the same thing as restraint.
agent-ops.whyExists: Agents create long traces, tool calls, permissions, side effects, retries, approvals, and costs. Ordinary application logs are rarely enough when the system can go wrong in six steps and a cheerful tone.

askNext: Which layers handle discovery, action, identity, permissions, and audit?
Where do trust boundaries exist between clients, servers, agents, and users?
Which protocol reduces integration work, and which one only moves responsibility into another box?
agent-protocol-stack.body: Stacks are useful because they separate concerns. They are dangerous when they also separate consequences.
agent-protocol-stack.example: "The agent protocol stack looked elegant until the audit log showed three systems agreeing that someone else had authorised the refund."
agent-protocol-stack.misuse: It is misused when teams point at a stack diagram as though the hard decisions are solved by having labelled boxes. Protocols can reduce bespoke glue; they cannot remove ownership, trust, failure handling, or the need to decide who is allowed to do what.
agent-protocol-stack.practicalMeaning: In practice it means architects must track which layer handles discovery, which handles action, which handles identity, which logs decisions, which enforces permissions, and which team receives the incident when the layers politely blame one another.

translations.label: architect meaning
translations.text: The protocol and control layers needed for agents to discover, delegate, authenticate, act, and log.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Less bespoke integration, plus more places where responsibility must be nailed down.

agent-protocol-stack.warningLabel: A stack diagram is not an accountability model.
agent-protocol-stack.whyExists: Agents do not operate in a vacuum. They need context, tools, credentials, delegation, observability, policy checks, and sometimes other agents. As these pieces standardise, the hidden integration layer becomes a stack rather than a pile of one-off adapters.

askNext: How does this agent behave when a tool fails, a dependency stalls, or the goal becomes ambiguous?
What evidence shows reliability across runs rather than competence in a single curated trace?
Which failures are recoverable automatically, and which must escalate or stop?
agent-reliability.body: Agent reliability is where product ambition meets the ancient disciplines of systems engineering. The excitement usually survives. The hand-waving usually does not.
agent-reliability.example: "The agent was impressive in rehearsal and catastrophic in production, which is another way of saying the reliability conversation had been postponed until the customers joined it."
agent-reliability.misuse: It gets confused with accuracy on a benchmark or elegance in a single trace. A reliable agent is not merely capable on a good day. It is bounded, observable, and recoverable when the real world behaves like itself.
agent-reliability.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means eval harnesses, trace review, timeout policy, retry discipline, rollback paths, tool health checks, escalation routes, and evidence that long-running workflows remain useful after the product team stops hovering over them.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The agent remains useful when the environment stops cooperating.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Evidence that the workflow can complete, recover, or stop sanely under live conditions.

agent-reliability.whyExists: Agents fail in compound ways. The model can reason badly, the tool can misbehave, the environment can change, the state can bloat, and the retry logic can turn optimism into repetition. Reliability exists as a term because "the model seemed clever" is not an operations metric.

askNext: What exactly is isolated, and what state is still shared?
Can secrets, logs, files, browser sessions, network calls, or tool side effects cross the boundary?
How is leakage tested before the agent receives useful permissions?
agent-sandbox-leakage.body: No diagram is attached because the built-in worktree diagram would narrow the problem to Git-style file isolation. This entry is about the wider set of sandbox leaks: credentials, networks, sessions, logs, and tools.
agent-sandbox-leakage.example: "The agent was safely sandboxed, apart from the production API token, the shared browser session, and the export tool it used with touching confidence."
agent-sandbox-leakage.misuse: It gets ignored when "sandboxed" is treated as a complete answer. A sandbox may isolate filesystem changes while still sharing tokens, internet access, browser sessions, package caches, logging streams, or tools with real external consequences.
agent-sandbox-leakage.practicalMeaning: In practice it means checking the unpleasant edges: copied environment files, inherited credentials, shared volumes, outbound network rules, persistent browser profiles, unredacted logs, tool scopes, and any command that turns a contained mistake into an external action.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: The isolation boundary exists, but something important can still cross it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent cannot write to the main directory, but it may still know the password and find the internet.

agent-sandbox-leakage.warningLabel: A sandbox boundary is only useful where it is actually enforced.
agent-sandbox-leakage.whyExists: Useful agents need somewhere to read, write, run code, browse, call tools, and test ideas without being handed full production access. Sandboxes provide that containment, but real workflows often share caches, secrets, mounts, logs, sessions, networks, or approval paths.

askNext: What exactly is included in one agent seat?
Is pricing based on agents, users, tasks, usage, outcomes, or whichever number is largest?
Who pays for the human oversight the seat quietly assumes?
agent-seat.body: No diagram is attached because the concept is commercial, not architectural. The agent seat is mostly a negotiation about what kind of work software is allowed to pretend it has replaced.
agent-seat.example: "The quote replaced five human seats with three agent seats and one new spreadsheet for tracking why the arithmetic felt haunted."
agent-seat.misuse: It is abused when a seat implies dependable labour capacity that the product has not earned. A paid agent seat may still be a bundle of workflows, model calls, approvals, and support tickets wearing a small badge that says "colleague".
agent-seat.practicalMeaning: In practice it means procurement must ask what counts as one agent, how many workflows it can run, which users may supervise it, what usage is included, and whether the vendor has simply found a more flattering word for quota.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: A billable unit for role-shaped software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Headcount pricing with fewer labour laws and more ambiguity about supervision.

agent-seat.warningLabel: If the invoice calls software a worker, ask whether the support burden has also been automated.
agent-seat.whyExists: Vendors need a way to charge for software that performs role-like work across teams or systems. Buyers also need a way to compare the cost of an agent against staff, outsourcing, usage fees, and the unpriced labour of supervising it.

askNext: Who authored the skill, and can we audit the instructions and scripts?
What triggers the skill, and what context or permissions does it receive?
Does the skill reduce repeated prompting, or hide a fragile workflow inside a nicer folder?
agent-skills.body: Skills are a healthy idea when treated as maintainable operational assets. They become less healthy when installed like browser extensions and trusted like saints.
agent-skills.example: "The finance skill helped the agent produce the right spreadsheet; the security review then asked why everyone had installed it with the serenity of a gift basket."
agent-skills.misuse: They get treated as plugins, permissions, or magic competence capsules. A skill can guide behaviour and provide tools, but it still needs trust boundaries, review, versioning, and a system that knows when not to use it.
agent-skills.practicalMeaning: In practice it means packaging domain knowledge so the agent can discover the skill cheaply, load detailed guidance only when needed, and run deterministic helper scripts or consult reference files without bloating every task.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A reusable onboarding pack for the agent, loaded only when the work calls for it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A filesystem-shaped way to make agents more specialised without stuffing every task with every instruction.

agent-skills.warningLabel: A skill is capability packaging, not proof the agent is safe or competent.
agent-skills.whyExists: They exist because agents need domain procedures and repeatable workflows, but putting every possible instruction into the main context window is expensive, noisy, and a reliable way to teach the model nothing in particular.

Which workflows overlap, conflict, or expose the same systems with different policies?
What is the process for retiring an agent once the novelty has gone and the permissions remain?
agent-sprawl.body: Agent sprawl is not proof that the company is advanced. It is proof that procurement got easier than architecture.
agent-sprawl.example: "The company did not have an agent strategy so much as agent weather, with local outbreaks and confused reporting lines."
agent-sprawl.misuse: It gets used to sneer at any experimentation at all. Some multiplication is healthy. Sprawl begins when the overlap, risk, and maintenance burden outgrow the visibility and discipline around them.
agent-sprawl.practicalMeaning: In practice it means duplicate agents answering the same question differently, unclear ownership when one breaks, overlapping permissions, rising spend, inconsistent policy handling, and a governance meeting that discovers the estate only after the estate has discovered payroll.

translations.label: leadership meaning
translations.text: Innovation is thriving, and control has politely left the building.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Too many overlapping agent workflows for the current level of ownership and policy discipline.

agent-sprawl.whyExists: Agent tooling has become easier to launch than to govern. Local teams can spin up useful workflows quickly, and those small wins accumulate into a larger estate of permissions, prompts, budgets, and duplicated behaviours that nobody intended to administer as a single system.

askNext: Which parts of the run are captured, and which are hidden or summarised away?
Can this trace be replayed, searched, and tied back to a user-visible outcome?
Does the trace expose tool arguments and approvals, or just model prose?
agent-trace.body: An agent trace is not the agent's soul. It is the paperwork left behind when the soul becomes a support ticket.
agent-trace.example: "The agent trace looked impressive until step four, where the system ignored the failed lookup and built a procurement recommendation out of manners."
agent-trace.misuse: It gets treated as proof of reasoning rather than evidence of process. A trace can be incomplete, selectively logged, or polished by the platform. It shows what was recorded, not necessarily every cause behind the behaviour.
agent-trace.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a timeline that developers, auditors, or operators can replay when a workflow fails, costs too much, calls the wrong tool, or produces an answer no one wants to defend from memory.

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: A run history that helps explain how the agent got from task to consequence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The thing everyone asks for once the agent has already done the interesting damage.

agent-trace.whyExists: Multi-step agent runs are hard to judge from the final answer alone. The trace gives reviewers a way to see whether the agent followed a sensible path, called the right tools, respected boundaries, and recovered from problems instead of accidentally succeeding.

askNext: What can the agent spend, where, and without asking again?
Can the wallet be revoked faster than the agent can make a bad decision?
Who reviews disputed transactions: support, finance, security, or hope?
agent-wallet.body: No diagram is attached because the useful shape is governance, not a loop. The dangerous part is not that the agent can call a payment function. It is that the organisation starts treating that as consent.
agent-wallet.example: "The agent wallet worked exactly as designed, which is why the team finally noticed that no one had designed a refund policy."
agent-wallet.misuse: It gets treated as if putting money behind an agent is merely a UX improvement. In reality it changes the risk model: mistaken purchases, fraud, policy bypass, vendor steering, refund handling, and blame assignment all become part of the product.
agent-wallet.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means prepaid balances, spending caps, merchant allowlists, approval thresholds, revocation controls, transaction logs, and a painfully explicit answer to who pays when the agent has been cleverly wrong.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A bounded way for software to pay without becoming the human's full financial shadow.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A new control surface for spending limits, approvals, identity, and blame.

agent-wallet.warningLabel: Once the agent can spend, hallucination becomes an accounting event.
agent-wallet.whyExists: Agents that can book travel, buy supplies, trade compute, or pay other services need something more precise than "use the user's card". A wallet can separate authority, budget, identity, approvals, and audit history from the human's everyday account.

askNext: What can the system actually do without a human quietly steering it?
Which tools, permissions, and recovery loops make it more than a branded chat box?
How much of the promised autonomy survives contact with production edge cases?
agent-washing.body: The surest sign of agent washing is grammatical confidence. The noun arrives first. The actual capability, if it exists, takes the slower route through product reality.
agent-washing.example: "The product demo revealed that the revolutionary agent was mostly a prompt, a webhook, and a person named Kyle."
agent-washing.misuse: The term itself can also be overused to dismiss legitimate progress. Some systems do plan, call tools, recover from failure, and complete multi-step work. The problem is not the category. It is the inflation.
agent-washing.practicalMeaning: In practice agent washing usually means a renamed assistant, a brittle sequence of API calls, or a human-heavy service wrapped in the language of autonomy and sold at a more heroic multiple.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Autonomy has entered the deck before it has entered the product.

agent-washing.warningLabel: A fair suspicion, but not a substitute for technical inspection.
agent-washing.whyExists: Because "agent" currently signals strategic importance, investor appetite, and higher perceived value in a way that "workflow helper" regrettably does not.

askNext: What exactly is standardised: discovery, task state, result format, authentication, or all four?
How are permissions, identity, and audit trails handled across agents?
Are we enabling composability, or merely creating distributed confusion with better packet structure?
agent2agent.body: Standards matter most when everyone is about to build the same messy thing in slightly incompatible ways.
agent2agent.example: "Agent2Agent made the handoff cleaner, which was useful, though it did not improve the quality of the thinking at either end."
agent2agent.misuse: It gets discussed as though inter-agent communication automatically creates competence, trust, or composability. It does not. A standard message shape cannot rescue a bad agent any more than a calendar invite can rescue a bad meeting.
agent2agent.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means one system can delegate a task to another specialised system without hard-coding every detail of the exchange. That can make ecosystems more flexible. It can also spread failure, ambiguity, and responsibility across a larger map.

translations.label: architect meaning
translations.text: A standard way for agentic systems to pass work and state between each other.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Fewer bespoke connectors, plus a new set of questions about trust and ownership.

agent2agent.whyExists: As agent systems multiply, teams do not want every handoff between assistants, services, and vendors to become a bespoke treaty drafted in JSON and regret. A common contract lowers that integration cost.

askNext: What autonomy does the system actually have?
Which steps are deterministic software, and which steps depend on model judgement?
Where are the stop conditions, approvals, and failure boundaries?
agentic-ai.body:
The good question is not whether a system is agentic. The good question is whether the added autonomy meaningfully improves outcomes over a simpler, more inspectable design.

If the answer is "mainly in the pitch deck", you have your diagnosis.
agentic-ai.example: "Their agentic AI platform turned out to be a sensible approval workflow with retrieval, a model, and three places the system could politely stop pretending to know."
agentic-ai.misuse: It is abused whenever vendors want ordinary automation to sound frontier-adjacent. A brittle chain of prompts can be sold as agentic. So can a dashboard feature that merely triggers three API calls. The word now frequently signals aspiration more than architecture.
agentic-ai.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means workflow software wrapped around a model: planning prompts, tool calls, retries, validation, memory, approval checkpoints, and cost controls. The question is not whether it feels agentic. The question is whether it is useful, bounded, and worth the extra failure surface.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Our software does more than one model call and would like a larger contract.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A workflow with model-driven decision points, sometimes useful, often overstated.

agentic-ai.warningLabel: Often a sales term first and a technical description second.
agentic-ai.whyExists: The term exists because some systems genuinely do more than answer once. They break tasks into steps, select tools, inspect outputs, and continue until they hit a stopping condition. That pattern deserved a label. It just did not deserve the entire investor imagination.

askNext: Can the agent only recommend, or can it reserve and purchase?
Which constraints are hard limits rather than preferences in nicer clothing?
How are merchant incentives, substitutions, and refunds audited?
agentic-commerce.body: The hard part is not making an agent find a product. The hard part is making it spend money in a way that still looks sane after finance asks for the receipt.
agentic-commerce.example: "The agentic commerce pilot saved three minutes per purchase and created a new committee for deciding whether software may choose office chairs."
agentic-commerce.misuse: The phrase is abused when ordinary personalisation, chat-based search, or affiliate recommendations are presented as delegated buying. A chatbot that says "you might like this" is not commerce with agency. It is merchandising with better manners.
agentic-commerce.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what the agent may compare, which merchants it can contact, what budgets apply, how substitutions are approved, and who is accountable when the cheapest option was also the least survivable one.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Let the agent turn preferences into a completed commercial action.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A buying workflow with enough authority to make procurement nervous and enough ambiguity to make vendors cheerful.

agentic-commerce.warningLabel: A recommendation engine does not become an authorised buyer because the checkout button moved off-screen.
agentic-commerce.whyExists: Commerce systems were built around people browsing, clicking, and checking out. Agents change the shape of the flow: they need product data, constraints, permissions, payment authority, audit trails, and a clear moment where suggestion becomes transaction.

askNext: Does access attach to the agent, the user, the task, the run, or some convenient blur of all four?
Which policies constrain tool choice, data access, external actions, and delegated authority?
Can access be narrowed or revoked without breaking every agent that once touched the workflow?
agentic-iam.body: No diagram is attached because the core issue is policy and attribution across time. A single flowchart would be too kind to the mess.
agentic-iam.example: "Agentic IAM arrived after the support agent used a perfectly valid token for a task no one had meant to authorise."
agentic-iam.misuse: It gets reduced to giving the agent a service account. That may create a credential, but it does not answer who authorised a specific action, which task justified it, how long the authority should last, or what policy stopped the agent from becoming enthusiastic in the wrong system.
agentic-iam.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agent identities, delegated authentication, just-in-time scopes, policy engines, approval gates, run-level audit trails, credential expiry, revocation, and access reviews that understand software can now be both caller and decision-maker.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: IAM that treats agents as controlled actors, not accidental shadows of human accounts.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Access control rebuilt around delegated action, scoped authority, and logs that can survive a postmortem.

agentic-iam.warningLabel: A service account is not an agentic access model.
agentic-iam.whyExists: Traditional IAM already handles users, groups, services, roles, and policies. Agentic systems add non-human actors with goals, tool choices, delegated user authority, temporary tasks, run-level context, and side effects that need attribution and control.

askNext: What retrieval decisions does the agent make that a fixed pipeline would not?
How are loop limits, source quality, and stopping conditions defined?
Does agentic retrieval improve measured answer quality enough to justify the extra cost and failure surface?
agentic-rag.body: Agentic RAG is useful when retrieval is genuinely investigative. It is less useful when a simple search pipeline has been given a cape and a larger bill.
agentic-rag.example: "The agentic RAG system answered the policy question by searching the handbook, checking the latest memo, rejecting an obsolete clause, and still needing an eval to prove this was not just expensive browsing."
agentic-rag.misuse: It is used as a prestige label for any RAG system with more than one step. Extra loops do not automatically improve grounding. They can add latency, cost, failure paths, and confidence in a more elaborate mistake.
agentic-rag.practicalMeaning: In practice it means query planning, retrieval tools, loop limits, reranking, source triangulation, provenance capture, fallback behaviour, and evaluations that prove the extra agency is earning its keep.

translations.label: builder meaning
translations.text: Let an agent decide how to retrieve, not just what to say after retrieval.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A retrieval loop that needs budgets, stopping rules, and evidence that the loop improves more than the slide deck.

agentic-rag.whyExists: Some questions are too complex for a single search. They need decomposition, multiple queries, source comparison, follow-up retrieval, and a decision about when the evidence is good enough to stop.

askNext: What facts does an assistant need to choose or cite us accurately?
Can agents verify price, availability, policy, and authority without scraping prose?
Are we optimising for being found, being trusted, or being selected?
agentic-seo.body: Agentic SEO is not the death of content. It is the death of content that was only ever there to impress a crawler with low standards.
agentic-seo.example: "The agentic SEO audit found 400 inspirational pages and no reliable way for a shopping agent to know whether the product was in stock."
agentic-seo.misuse: It gets abused when old SEO tricks are renamed for agents. Keyword stuffing does not become strategy because the reader is a model. If anything, the model is less interested in your adjectives and more likely to punish your missing data.
agentic-seo.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schema, feeds, product truth, citation-worthy pages, API access, source reputation, freshness, permissions, and proof that an agent can complete the task without inventing half the offer.

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: Make your site legible to assistants, answer engines, and purchasing agents.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Clean operational data doing the work that landing-page perfume used to claim.

agentic-seo.warningLabel: Agents do not need your keyword density. They need trustworthy facts they can act on.
agentic-seo.whyExists: It exists because discovery is moving from human search results to assistant-mediated answers and actions. Machines need clean facts, structured data, availability, policies, provenance, and decision-ready evidence, not another paragraph about passion.

askNext: Which tasks would this system need to perform reliably across unfamiliar domains?
What evidence goes beyond benchmark spikes and polished demos?
Who decides that the threshold has been crossed, and by what standard?
agi.body: AGI remains useful as a provocation and dangerous as a schedule. Once it enters a room, timelines harden, valuations inflate, and ordinary questions about reliability start dressing more modestly.
agi.example: "The deck mentioned AGI on slide three, which saved everyone the trouble of asking how the product planned to make money this year."
agi.misuse: By treating it as a launch category, a press strategy, or a vague aura that descends on any model after a particularly strong demo. AGI is a very large claim, not a mood.
agi.note: People use AGI to mean slightly different things: human-level performance on most cognitive tasks, economically transformative autonomy, or merely "the models got unsettlingly competent". The ambiguity is part of the term's power.
agi.practicalMeaning: In practice the term often functions as strategic theatre: it shapes fundraising, policy lobbying, hiring plans, and media attention long before anyone agrees on what threshold would actually count.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A future-facing claim that lets today's systems borrow tomorrow's mystique.

agi.warningLabel: Usually a forecast dressed as a noun.
agi.whyExists: The term exists because narrow systems can be impressive without being general, and people need a name for the stronger claim that a machine could transfer competence broadly rather than merely excel on selected tasks.

askNext: What permissions does the app receive when installed?
Who reviewed the instructions, tools, data access, and update path?
Can the platform remove or quarantine a dangerous listing after users depend on it?
ai-app-store.body: The old app store asked whether software would run on your phone. The AI app store has to ask what the software will persuade your agent to do.
ai-app-store.example: "The AI app store promised a procurement assistant, then asked for inbox access with the modesty of a nightclub bouncer."
ai-app-store.misuse: It gets abused when listing becomes credibility. An AI app, agent, or skill may carry instructions, tool access, data permissions, hidden maintenance risk, and a cheerful description written by the party most excited about adoption.
ai-app-store.practicalMeaning: In practice it means review processes, permissions, versioning, security scanning, developer identity, revenue share, uninstall paths, data access disclosures, and a policy for removing agents that are popular for the wrong reasons.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A distribution shelf for installable AI capabilities.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An ecosystem play whose trust boundary arrives bundled with the listing.

ai-app-store.warningLabel: A marketplace listing is not a security review.
ai-app-store.whyExists: It exists because platforms want ecosystems, developers want distribution, and users want reusable capabilities without building every integration themselves. The app-store shape is familiar; the trust model is less comforting.

askNext: What does the browser genuinely do that extensions or a pinned chat window do not?
How much browsing data is being used to power the assistant features?
Is the real play productivity, search distribution, or user lock-in?
ai-browser.body: The browser wars have returned with a new claim: this time the winner will be whichever tab learns to speak first.
ai-browser.example: "The AI browser promised a calmer web and delivered a more conversational way to accumulate open tabs."
ai-browser.misuse: It is sold as a reinvention of the web when it is often a conventional browser wrapped around a sidebar, some workflow shortcuts, and a larger ambition about owning the default surface.
ai-browser.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means built-in page summaries, tab understanding, search assistance, form filling, shopping help, and occasional task automation. It may also mean a fresh argument about privacy, telemetry, and who gets to mediate your view of the internet.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Put the assistant inside the browser so help, search, and automation are always one click away.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The browser wants to be the operating system for your next prompt.

ai-browser.warningLabel: A convenience layer and a distribution battle, often at the same time.
ai-browser.whyExists: The browser remains a strategic surface for search, discovery, work, and distribution. If assistants are going to sit anywhere all day, vendors would prefer that somewhere to be under their own chrome.

askNext: What kind of attachment is the product encouraging, whether it admits it or not?
How are memory, intimacy, and boundaries managed over long-running chats?
What happens when a vulnerable user treats generated attention as judgement or truth?
ai-companion.body: The companion category matters because it exposes a quiet ambition in consumer AI: not merely to answer questions, but to occupy time, mood, and eventually routine.
ai-companion.example: "It began as an experiment in conversational UX and ended with product analytics reporting a disturbingly loyal evening audience."
ai-companion.misuse: By pretending the relationship layer is incidental or harmless. Attachment is not a side effect here. It is part of the product design, and that changes the safety questions.
ai-companion.practicalMeaning: In practice AI companion products optimise for persona consistency, frequent engagement, memory cues, and emotional tone, while trying to manage the risks of dependency, manipulation, or delusion amplification.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A relationship-shaped interface built on prediction, persona management, and habit formation.

ai-companion.warningLabel: Engagement design becomes more consequential when the product can impersonate care.
ai-companion.whyExists: Because people want attention, conversation, flirtation, rehearsal, reassurance, and low-friction company, and software has discovered that these are recurring use cases with excellent retention mechanics.

askNext: Which controls belong in the gateway, and which belong in the application?
Can the gateway fail open, fail closed, or fail expensively?
Does routing improve quality and cost, or merely move complexity into a nicer dashboard?
ai-gateway.body: An AI gateway is not glamorous infrastructure, which is why it is often useful. The glamour returns only when the invoice arrives with charts.
ai-gateway.example: "The AI gateway earned its keep when the provider outage became a routing event instead of a company-wide treasure hunt for hard-coded API keys."
ai-gateway.misuse: It gets marketed as an instant platform. A gateway can centralise traffic, but it cannot decide product quality, fix bad prompts, or turn unclear governance into architecture by standing in the middle.
ai-gateway.practicalMeaning: In practice it means one place to manage API keys, provider routing, request metadata, redaction, quotas, fallbacks, and dashboards. It also means one more critical dependency that must be boringly reliable.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Put model traffic through one controlled doorway.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The place where security, finance, latency, and developer convenience are invited to disagree in public.

ai-gateway.whyExists: Teams need a common control point for many applications, models, providers, and budgets. A gateway makes it possible to change providers, enforce policy, observe usage, and prevent every product team from inventing its own fragile little tunnel.

askNext: What authority does the office have to approve, block, remediate, or retire systems?
Which inventories, evaluations, incidents, and vendor reviews does it actually maintain?
How does it help teams ship responsibly instead of converting risk into paperwork?
ai-governance-office.body: An AI governance office earns its name when it changes decisions. Before that, it is a calendar event with institutional aspirations.
ai-governance-office.example: "The AI governance office stopped being decorative when it could pause a launch rather than merely admire the risk register."
ai-governance-office.misuse: It becomes theatre when it owns templates but not authority, meetings but not telemetry, and principles but not the ability to block, remediate, or retire risky systems.
ai-governance-office.practicalMeaning: In practice it means maintaining model inventories, review gates, risk tiers, acceptable-use rules, vendor assessments, evaluation standards, incident workflows, audit evidence, and escalation paths that people can follow without needing a priesthood.

translations.label: organisation meaning
translations.text: The function that turns AI policy into ownership, review, controls, and escalation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful control body, unless it is only a meeting room with a vocabulary.

ai-governance-office.whyExists: AI deployments cross legal, security, product, data, procurement, HR, and operational boundaries. A dedicated governance function can make risk ownership, approval paths, standards, and incident response visible before every team invents its own private constitution.

askNext: What stays defensible if model quality equalises across suppliers?
Does the advantage come from distribution, data, workflow lock-in, or simply being early?
How quickly could a competitor copy the visible product without copying the hard-earned operating knowledge?
ai-moat.body: If the moat lives entirely inside a shared model API, bring a sturdier drawbridge.
ai-moat.example: "Their AI moat turned out to be a paid API account and an unusually ornate seed deck."
ai-moat.misuse: By implying that mere model access is itself a moat. Using a frontier API is not a moat. Adding chat to a familiar workflow is not automatically a moat either. Sometimes the only durable thing in the room is the slide about defensibility.
ai-moat.practicalMeaning: In practice a real moat usually lives in distribution, embedded workflow, proprietary data feedback loops, operational excellence, trust, switching costs, or an ability to make the economics work after the demo glow fades.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: The part of the pitch that answers what happens once everyone else has access to similar models.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Something outside the model itself that might still matter when the underlying capability becomes cheaper.

ai-moat.whyExists: Because investors and operators need a way to distinguish temporary novelty from lasting advantage. In AI markets, model capability moves quickly, so the question of what remains defensible arrives earlier and less politely.

ai-psychosis.body:
The phrase is useful only if it stays disciplined. It should point to distorted interpretation around AI, not become a lazy way to pathologise disagreement.

If a conversation needs clinical language, use clinical language. If it needs cultural criticism, use this term with that narrower ambition.
ai-psychosis.example: "After a week with the chatbot, he was speaking as if the product had opinions, trauma, and a five-year strategy. The rest of the team called it AI Psychosis and gently took the API keys away."
ai-psychosis.misuse: It gets abused as a cheap insult for anyone enthusiastic about AI, which makes it less useful. Genuine interest in the field is not the same thing as delusional over-interpretation. The phrase is most credible when it points to exaggerated belief formation, not mere excitement or scepticism.
ai-psychosis.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone has stopped evaluating systems by evidence and started treating outputs, roadmaps, or rumours as proof of exceptional intelligence, moral authority, or historical inevitability. It can show up as compulsive prompting, anthropomorphism, policy overreaction, or investment logic built on vibes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone has stopped distinguishing between a persuasive interface and a reliable understanding of what the system is doing.

ai-psychosis.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a cultural term, not a psychiatric diagnosis.
ai-psychosis.whyExists: People need a label for the strange social effect AI systems can have on users, founders, pundits, and investors: obsession, magical thinking, and the loss of proportion. The term exists because "you are taking autocomplete far too personally" was too long for social media.

ai-roll-up.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means some real workflow improvement, some software unification, some headcount pressure, and a great many founders discovering that regional service businesses have more edge cases than a prompt can politely absorb.

ai-roll-up.whyExists: It exists because many industries remain fragmented, process-heavy, and badly digitised. That creates a tempting thesis: buy the businesses, modernise the workflows, and collect the efficiency gains before anyone else learns to describe them as inevitable.

askNext: What action is blocked until approval is granted?
What evidence does the approver see, and can they meaningfully reject the action?

approval-gate.body: No diagram is attached here because a gate is only useful when its surrounding authority is clear. A diagram would make the latch look more impressive than the lock.
approval-gate.example: "The approval gate stopped the agent from sending the refund email, though it could do nothing about the three-paragraph apology it had already composed for morale."
approval-gate.misuse: It gets treated as safety by interface design. A button labelled "approve" is not a meaningful gate if the reviewer lacks context, cannot reject without penalty, or is asked after the important action has already happened.
approval-gate.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means risk thresholds, reviewer context, audit records, timeout behaviour, rejection paths, escalation rules, and a clear distinction between approval as control and approval as decoration.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A control that can actually stop a risky action before it happens.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A pause point whose value depends entirely on what the approver can see and do.

approval-gate.whyExists: Agent workflows can read data, call tools, spend money, send messages, change records, or trigger downstream systems. Approval gates exist because some actions deserve a pause, evidence, and a responsible decision before automation turns possibility into fact.

approval-theatre.body: Approval theatre is what happens when the approval artefact matters more than the approval decision. The paperwork flourishes precisely as the control weakens.
approval-theatre.example: "The agent had three approval steps, two screenshot exports, and absolutely no stage at which anyone could still say no. Approval theatre was having a very productive quarter."
approval-theatre.misuse: Not every lightweight approval path is theatre. Fast approvals can still be real if the reviewer sees the right evidence and can meaningfully block the action. The phrase earns its keep when the approval is decorative, rushed, or structurally toothless.
approval-theatre.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means unread dashboards, one-click sign-offs, ritual thumbs-up messages, reviewers with no context, and nominal gates that cannot stop the system because the incentives, access, or deadlines have already decided the outcome.

arthur-mensch.body: When a founder becomes a stand-in for continental ambition, every product announcement begins carrying a diplomatic undertone.
arthur-mensch.example: "They cited Arthur less as a founder and more as a geopolitical exhibit for why Europe did not have to outsource all of its ambition."
arthur-mensch.misuse: By turning Mistral into either a romantic openness story or a simple anti-Big Tech morality play. The reality is more strategic: capability, optionality, regulation, distribution, and enterprise positioning are all in the room.
arthur-mensch.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Arthur Mensch, they usually mean the possibility that serious model-building can happen outside the dominant US lab pattern, and that sovereignty itself has become part of the AI argument.

translations.text: A signal that AI strategy has acquired a map.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Mistral's role in Europe's attempt to matter directly in model building, not merely in regulation.

arthur-mensch.whyExists: Because AI has a geography problem as well as a compute problem. Mensch's name often gets used as shorthand for European model ambition, strategic independence, and a less concentrated map of where frontier capability can come from.

askNext: What source material is the answer actually grounded in?

ask-maps.body: Maps products have always wanted to be more than navigation. Ask Maps is simply the first version confident enough to speak in full sentences about it.
ask-maps.example: "Ask Maps was excellent at understanding the question, which left the older problem of trusting the answer exactly where it was."
ask-maps.misuse: It is described as local intelligence when it is still a synthesis layer built on listings, reviews, platform incentives, and model judgement. The interface becomes more humane. The uncertainty does not retire.
ask-maps.practicalMeaning: In practice it means conversational local search, quicker trip planning, reservation prompts, and one more surface where the user is encouraged to ask in prose while seeing less of the underlying source material directly.

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: A way to ask the map the actual question instead of reverse-engineering it into filters.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Conversational local search with a friendlier interface and the same old need to verify the details.

ask-maps.whyExists: People do not think in tidy filter chips. They think in awkward constraints: chargers, queues, children, parking, weather, lighting, and whether the coffee is worth the detour. Ask Maps exists to make those messy requests legible to the product.
